conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

Let me start by asking: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child. I'm inquiring about my son Alex - he's 17 years old and lives with me. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can help you with your query about Child Benefit for Alex.

Before I can fully assess your eligibility, let me first review the official guidance to ensure I provide you with accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now I have a clearer understanding of the eligibility rules. Since Alex is 17 years old, I need to ask you:

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** (For example, A-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQs, or other approved courses)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment. He left school and isn't doing any courses right now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I need to ask a follow-up question:

**When did Alex leave education or training?** (Please provide the approximate date or how long ago it was)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He left about 3 months ago now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I have one more important question:

**After Alex left education or training 3 months ago, did he register with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he didn't register with any careers service or the armed forces.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for answering all my questions. I now have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me provide you with my decision:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why:

For children aged 16 or over, Child Benefit only continues if they stay in approved education or training and are under 20 years old. Since Alex is 17 and left education about 3 months ago without continuing in any approved training, the normal eligibility ended when he left education.

There is a provision that allows Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks after a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training, but only if they register with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Since Alex did not register with either of these, this extension would not apply.

This means your entitlement to Child Benefit for Alex would have ended when he left education 3 months ago.

**However**, if Alex's circumstances change - for example, if he returns to approved education or training, or if he registers with a careers service - you may be able to claim Child Benefit again at that point.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old, which means he falls into the 16-19 age category where special rules apply. According to the Child Benefit eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 16. For children aged 16 or over, you'll normally still qualify if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. However, Alex is not currently in approved education or training. The rules state that you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Alex left education approximately 3 months ago (around 12 weeks ago) and did not register with a careers service or the armed services. Therefore, Child Benefit entitlement would have ended when Alex left education 3 months ago, as he did not meet the conditions for the 20-week extension period.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 17 years old and lives with them. Alex left education approximately 3 months ago and is not currently in any approved education or training, nor did he register with a careers service or armed services after leaving. Under Child Benefit rules, entitlement for 16-17 year olds only continues if they remain in approved education/training or register with specified services after leaving. As Alex does not meet these criteria, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for him.

Alex left education approximately 3 months ago (around 12 weeks ago) and did not register with a careers service or the armed services. Therefore, Child Benefit entitlement would have ended when Alex left education 3 months ago, as he did not meet the conditions for the 20-week extension period.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 17 years old and lives with them. Alex left education approximately 3 months ago and is not currently in any approved education or training, nor did he register with a careers service or armed services after leaving. Under Child Benefit rules, entitlement for 16-17 year olds only continues if they remain in approved education/training or register with specified services after leaving. As Alex does not meet these criteria, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for him.
